On the occasion of the premiere of Woman King, National Geographic has dedicated a face lift, or rather a blood wash, to this tribe of slavers who were the Dahomey, more specifically to the fanatic, slit throat fat women who blindly served their wren. The horrors that these women practiced had no limit, from annihilating villages at night, carrying out monstrous human sacrifices, and even hunting the elephants in the area to extinction. This is how they were spent by a kingdom whose main trade was the sale of slaves to the hungry European powers, but also for its own use, because in this kingdom, whose throne was decorated with human skulls, about the 90% were slaves. None of this will be seen in the movie, nor in the pathetic Geographic article, where you can read "larger than life", "empowered" and other paid nonsense. We are going downhill if we make heros of murderers, idols of the psychopaths and liberators of the slavers. The legend of the invincible female warriors, who made up only a sixth of the royal army and who had already suffered many defeats at the hands of other tribes, ended when they messed with the French, who did not have a hard time crushing them: heavily outnumbered they suffered hardly any casualties; in contrast,  the Dahomey amazons were all but wiped out. The Dahomey Amazons (Fon: "Agojie", "Agoji", "Mino", or "Minon") were a Fon all-female military regiment of the Kingdom of Dahomey which existed from the 1600s until 1904.
The emergence of an all-female military regiment was the result of Dahomey's male population facing high casualties in the increasingly frequent violence and warfare with neighbouring West African states. This led to Dahomey being one of the leading tribes in the slave trade with the Oyo Empire, which used slaves for commodity exchange in West Africa until the British Empire brought an end to the slave trade in the region. The lack of men likely led the kings of Dahomey to recruit women into the army.[2]

There are accounts of Dahomey warriors conducting slave raids on villages where they cut the heads off of the elderly and rip the bottom jaw bones off others. Nzinga was born between 1581/82, years after the arrival in the region of the Portuguese navigator Paulo Dias de Novais (1560) and the foundation of the city of Luanda (1575), in a context of struggles and resistance against Portuguese occupation. Daughter of Ngola Mbande Kiluanji, King of Ndongo, proved to be an excellent negotiator when her brother, King Ngola Mabande's successor, sent her to Luanda, one of the largest export centers for enslaved people on the African continent, in order to negotiate a peace treaty. that would establish respect for the sovereignty of the kingdom. Nzinga knew the Portuguese language and culture well, thanks to her contacts with missionaries and traders who passed through Ndongo.
